did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) based on the GoogLeNet architecture to classify clinical images of skin tumors into 14 different diagnoses. The AI system was trained on 4867 images and achieved 96.3% sensitivity and 89.5% specificity in skin cancer detection, outperforming board-certified dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document explicitly states 'Article type : Original Article' in the header. The study presents original experimental results, including the development and training of a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) on a specific dataset of 4867 images, and reports new performance metrics (96.3% sensitivity, 89.5% specificity) compared against board-certified dermatologists.

title: Deep learning-based, computer-aided classifier developed with a small dataset of clinical images surpasses board-certified dermatologists in skin tumor diagnosis
brief_summary: This study developed a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) based on the GoogLeNet architecture to classify clinical images of skin tumors into 14 diagnoses using a dataset of 4867 images from 1842 patients at the University of Tsukuba Hospital. The system was evaluated against 13 board-certified dermatologists and 9 trainees using a test set of 1142 images, achieving an overall classification accuracy of 76.5%. In detecting skin cancer (keratinocyte cancer and melanoma), the DCNN demonstrated 96.3% sensitivity and 89.5% specificity, outperforming the board-certified dermatologists who achieved 85.3% accuracy. The model showed robustness with a fluctuation range of only ±3.2% in five-fold cross-validation. The authors conclude that the DCNN can classify skin tumors more accurately than board-certified dermatologists using a single clinical image, suggesting potential for screening in general medical practice after prospective validation.

all_task_types.target: 14 different diagnoses of skin tumors (malignant melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, Bowen disease, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, nevus cell nevus, blue nevus, congenital melanocytic nevus, Spitz nevus, sebaceous nevus, poroma, seborrheic keratosis, nevus spilus, lentigo simplex) and binary classification of malignant vs. benign conditions.
all_task_types.details: The DCNN classifies clinical images into 21 disease classes (taxonomy tree) or 14 diagnoses. The primary task is discrete diagnostic categorization. Performance metrics include sensitivity (96.3%) and specificity (89.5%) for skin cancer detection, and overall classification accuracy (76.5% at 3rd level). The model outputs Top-1 accuracy and class probabilities to inform biopsy decisions, but the core task is classification, not regression.

datasets_used.name: University of Tsukuba Hospital Clinical Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private, retrospective dataset of digital clinical images of skin tumors and pigmented skin lesions extracted from the database of the Dermatology Division of the University of Tsukuba Hospital, collected from 2003 to 2016. Images were taken with digital cameras (at least 6 million pixels, macro lens, macro ring flash) without dermoscopy. Diagnoses were based on pathological examination for most cases, except congenital melanocytic nevus, nevus spilus, and lentigo simplex.

use.training: Used to train the Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) based on GoogLeNet architecture. Data augmentation included rotation (24x) and random fluctuation of blur and brightness.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the DCNN's classification accuracy and compare it against board-certified dermatologists and trainees. A subset of 140 images was randomly selected for pairwise comparison with dermatologists.
use.other: Used for five-fold cross-validation to verify model robustness.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To verify the robustness of the DCNN model and assess the fluctuation range of classification accuracy.
To compare the classification accuracy of the DCNN against board-certified dermatologists and dermatology trainees.
To determine statistical significance for the comparisons between DCNN and human readers.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in classification accuracy between the DCNN and the human readers (board-certified dermatologists or dermatology trainees).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in classification accuracy between the DCNN and the human readers (specifically, that the DCNN's accuracy is statistically higher than that of the board-certified dermatologists).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study used P < .05 as the significance threshold. It reported that the DCNN achieved significantly higher accuracy than board-certified dermatologists for both 1st level classification (P < .0001) and 3rd level classification (P < .0001). It also reported that board-certified dermatologists had significantly higher accuracy than trainees (P < .01).

University of Tsukuba Hospital Clinical.notes: The DCNN achieved significantly higher accuracy than board-certified dermatologists for both 1st level (P < .0001) and 3rd level (P < .0001) classification. Board-certified dermatologists had significantly higher accuracy than trainees (P < .01). The DCNN's 3rd level accuracy fluctuation range in 5-fold cross-validation was ± 3.2%.

Specific Lesion Classification Results (.actinic_keratosis: < 80% accuracy (28 of 30 misclassified as other epithelial malignancies)

architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained on the 1000 object classes (1.2 million images) of the LSVRC 2012 classification dataset, achieving 72.1% Top-1 accuracy.
architecture_used.input_size: 1000 by 1000 pixels (images were trimmed to this size to center the objective tumor).

architecture_details.data_augmentation: Rotation by 15-degree increments (24x increase), random blur filter fluctuation (0 to 5 pixels), and brightness fluctuation (-10% to +10%).

training_details.robustness_metric: Fluctuation range of ± 3.2% in 3rd level classification accuracy

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) based on GoogLeNet architecture
Traditional machine learning classifiers (referenced as baseline/comparison in literature review)
preprocessing_techniques: Trimming images to 1000 by 1000 pixels to center the objective tumor
Random fluctuation of blur filter (0 to 5 pixels)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Each training image was rotated by 15-degree increments, increasing the number of training images by 24 times.
Blur filter was randomly fluctuated from 0 to 5 pixels during training.
Brightness was randomly fluctuated from -10% to +10% during training.

conclusions: A deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) trained on a relatively small dataset of 4867 clinical images from 1842 patients can classify images of skin tumors into 14 different diagnoses more accurately than board-certified dermatologists.
The DCNN achieved 96.3% sensitivity and 89.5% specificity in the detection of skin cancer (keratinocyte cancer and melanoma).
The system demonstrated robustness with a fluctuation range of only ± 3.2% in five-fold cross-validation for 3rd level classification accuracy.
The DCNN requires only a single clinical image for classification, suggesting potential utility for screening purposes in general medical practice.

The system has limitations regarding its inability to process inflammatory skin conditions and its reliance solely on clinical images, which may lead to misclassification of certain lesions (e.g., poroma, seborrheic keratosis) due to shared features with other malignancies.

The study cohort had a higher risk of malignancy and a specific distribution of acral melanoma compared to general medicine clinics, indicating that sensitivity and specificity might reduce with different datasets due to patient selection bias.
Prospective clinical validation is required before considering the system's use for screening purposes in general medical practice.
Future improvements may involve combining the system with dermoscopy diagnosis systems or utilizing DCNN output probabilities to inform biopsy decisions for borderline lesions.
important_remarks: The study cohort had a higher risk of malignancy and a specific distribution of acral melanoma (52.6% of MM patients) compared to general medicine clinics, indicating potential patient selection bias that may reduce sensitivity and specificity in different datasets.
The system was not capable of processing inflammatory skin conditions as only skin tumors and related conditions were included in the dataset.

The system relies solely on clinical images without dermoscopy, which limits diagnostic accuracy for lesions with shared features (e.g., poroma, seborrheic keratosis) and may lead to misclassification.
Prospective clinical validation is required before considering the system's use for screening purposes in general medical practice.

The classification was based on a single clinical image; dermatologists might achieve higher accuracy if provided with additional clinical information such as clinical course, size, location, tactility, or dermoscopic images.
The study was funded by none declared, but authors had conflicts of interest as some were employed by Kyocera Communication System Co., Ltd.
The dataset was collected from a single center (University of Tsukuba Hospital) between 2003 and 2016, limiting external generalizability.
